title: Linear representations of Kahler groups
speaker: Philippe Eyssidieux
field_msc: 32 Several complex variables and analytic spaces
tags: Workshop on Fundamental Groups and Periods

proof_stages.label: Kähler-group representation setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk narrows Serre's problem for fundamental groups of complex projective or compact Kähler manifolds to infinite compact Kähler groups equipped with a complex linear representation rho, with H later taken as ker(rho).

proof_stages.label: Campana-Corlette Shafarevich fibration and regular model
proof_stages.summary: The H-Shafarevich fibration is defined as the meromorphic fibration contracting precisely those very general subspaces whose induced pi_1-image in pi_1(X)/H is finite, and the stronger regular model is formulated to rule out special-fiber pathologies.

proof_stages.label: Semisimple monodromy theorem
proof_stages.summary: For representations whose Zariski closure is semisimple, Zuo's theorem and refinements give existence of the rho-Shafarevich morphism, general type of its target after a torsion-free finite étale cover, and factorization of rho through that target, using Kodaira-dimension additivity and the structure of subvarieties finite over tori.

proof_stages.label: Reductive factorization through general type and torus factors
proof_stages.summary: The reductive case is reduced to semisimple and toric pieces, giving after finite étale cover a factorization of the representation through a product of a general-type Shafarevich factor and an Albanese torus.

proof_stages.label: Arbitrary linear representation structure theorem
proof_stages.summary: Without assumptions on the Zariski closure, a finite étale cover has Shafarevich variety bimeromorphic to a smooth fibration whose base is of general type and whose fibers are complex tori, with rho factoring through it; the proof combines Katzarkov's unipotent case, a relative version of that argument, and Kollár-Nakayama smoothness.

proof_stages.label: Projective realization of linear Kähler monodromy
proof_stages.summary: The smooth torus-fibration structure is used, after finite étale cover and construction of a section, to deform the fibration first to a Jacobian fibration and then to one with abelian-variety fibers, producing a complex projective manifold with commensurable monodromy and yielding a finite-index projective subgroup for any linear Kähler group.

examples.name: Nori-chain pathology
examples.note: Nori chains are invoked as the pathology showing why the stronger regular Shafarevich model must test all compact connected analytic subspaces, not just normal ones through very general points.
examples.engagement_reason: the Nori-chain pathology is only invoked as a strange behavior to be excluded, without constructing or analyzing such a chain

passes.reason: A non-normal/reducible Nori chain is invoked as the special-fiber pathology showing that a normal very-general test would miss bad cases.
Nori chains are reducible/non-normal pathological chains invoked to show that testing only normal spaces would miss failures at special points.
Nori chains are singular/reducible chain pathologies invoked to show the naive normal/very-general contraction condition can fail.
A singular/non-normal Nori chain is invoked as a pathology showing that a naive normal or very-general testing condition would fail.
a reducible non-normal chain pathology is used to show that testing only normal subspaces or using a naive definition is insufficient

used as a pathological non-normal/reducible chain showing that weaker Shafarevich-model tests would miss special-fiber behavior
a reducible/non-normal Nori chain is invoked as a pathology showing the naive normal-subspace test would fail
a singular/reducible Nori chain is invoked as the pathology showing that a normal-only or very-general Shafarevich condition is insufficient
Nori chains are invoked as a singular/reducible pathology obstructing a naive normal-only Shafarevich contraction condition.
used as a non-normal/reducible chain pathology showing a naive normal-only Shafarevich condition would fail
Non-normal reducible Nori chains are invoked as the pathology showing that a normal-only test would miss required contractions.

examples.name: Albanese Stein factorization for the commutator subgroup
examples.note: When H is the commutator subgroup of pi_1(X), the Stein factorization of the Albanese map is presented as a concrete instance of the H-Shafarevich morphism.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the Albanese map and its Stein factorization to the commutator subgroup case and identifies the resulting fibration as the Shafarevich morphism

passes.reason: The Albanese Stein factorization realizes the universal/maximal abelian quotient given by the commutator subgroup, and is used as a standard illustration.
The commutator subgroup gives the maximal abelian quotient, realized geometrically by the Albanese Stein factorization.
The Albanese Stein factorization for the commutator subgroup is a standard positive model of the Shafarevich morphism.

the Albanese Stein factorization realizes the universal maximal abelian quotient and is offered as an illustrative case

given as a positive standard example, with the commutator kernel realizing the maximal abelian quotient via the Albanese Stein factorization

the commutator subgroup gives the maximal abelian quotient realized by the Albanese Stein factorization, used as a positive model example

a positive standard example where the commutator kernel gives the maximal abelian/Albanese quotient via Stein factorization
The Albanese Stein factorization is the universal maximal abelian-quotient case for the commutator subgroup and is used as a standard illustration.

examples.engagement_reason: the speaker concretely runs the period-map construction for a polarized integral variation of Hodge structure and takes its Stein factorization to obtain the Shafarevich morphism

examples.name: A Shafarevich variety equal to P^1
examples.note: The possible occurrence of P^1 as a Shafarevich variety is used to illustrate why finite covers are only finite on coarse varieties unless one keeps the appropriate Deligne-Mumford stack/orbifold structure.
examples.engagement_reason: the possibility that the Shafarevich variety is P^1 is stated as a simple example, with only brief comments about stack structure and no worked construction

passes.reason: A Shafarevich variety equal to genus-zero P^1 is a minimal highly symmetric curve, used to show the ordinary variety need not have the expected general-type behavior without stack/torsion-free structure.
The coarse Shafarevich variety being P^1 is a genus-zero, highly symmetric extreme case used to show that a naive general-type conclusion can fail without the torsion-free/stack refinement.
A Shafarevich variety equal to the highly symmetric minimal-genus curve P^1 is used to show the naive general-type statement can fail without the torsion/stack refinement.
P^1 is the genus-zero maximally symmetric curve and is used to show the coarse Shafarevich variety need not satisfy the general-type or étale expectation.
P^1 is the maximally homogeneous minimal-genus curve and is invoked to refute an unqualified étale or general-type expectation for the coarse Shafarevich variety
P^1 is the maximally symmetric genus-zero curve and is used to refute an étale or general-type expectation without stack structure
used to refute a naive étale/general-type expectation, and P^1 is the genus-zero extremal smooth projective curve
the genus-zero variety P^1 is a highly symmetric extremal curve cited to show the unstacked Shafarevich variety need not have the general-type behavior
P^1 is the maximally homogeneous genus-zero curve and is cited to show that the coarse Shafarevich variety need not be general type or étale without stack/torsion-free structure
A Shafarevich variety equal to P^1 is used to show torsion-free or stack qualifications are necessary, and P^1 is the genus-zero extremal curve.
invoked to show the underlying Shafarevich variety need not have the hoped-for general-type/étale behavior without stack or torsion-free structure
Underlying P^1 is the genus-zero homogeneous projective curve and is cited to refute the naive unstacked general-type/étale expectation.

examples.name: Base-point case: a complex torus deforms to an abelian variety
examples.note: The special case where the smooth torus fibration has a point as base makes the later deformation-to-projective-fibers argument transparent.
examples.engagement_reason: the base-point case of a complex torus deforming to an abelian variety is cited as well known and easy, but no deformation argument is carried out for that case

passes.reason: The fibration base has collapsed to a point, giving the degenerate base case where the total space is just a complex torus deforming to an abelian variety.
The base being a point is the minimal fibration case, used as a benign base case for deforming a torus to an abelian variety.
The base-point case collapses the torus fibration to the minimal case of a single complex torus, used constructively in the deformation argument.
The fibration over a point is the collapsed base case, used constructively as the starting deformation case.
the fibration over a point is the trivial minimal-base case used as the easy starting case
the base equal to a point is the collapsed minimal fibration case used as a harmless base case for deformation
used as the base case of the deformation argument, where the fibration base is a point and the torus is translation-homogeneous
the base is a point, collapsing the relative torus-fibration situation to the trivial absolute deformation case
the zero-dimensional base is the minimal boundary case of the torus fibration, used as an easy positive deformation case
The base being a point is a collapsed base case of the torus-fibration deformation argument.
the fibration over a point is the trivial base case used positively in the deformation argument
The base over a point is the trivial minimal fibration case used as the easy deformation starting point.
